title: Move Over, Chimps: This Cow Uses a Brush as a Tool — and Scientists Are Stunned
summary: When Veronika gets an itch, she doesn't just rub against a fence like most cows. Instead, this Brown Swiss cow from a small farm in Austria picks up a long-handled deck brush with her tongue, twists her neck, and scratches the exact spot that needs attention. That alone would be impressive — but Veronika goes further. She uses both ends of the brush. For tough, thick skin on her back, she uses the hard bristles. For sensitive areas like her belly button or udder, she carefully uses the soft handle. Sometimes she even grips the brush in the middle so she can switch ends without having to regrip. "At the beginning, I thought that it was a mistake" when she used the handle, said Antonio Osuna-Mascaró, an animal cognition expert at the Messerli Research Institute in Vienna. "But after observing Veronika for a little while, it was so obvious that she was using both tool ends in different ways." This makes Veronika the first cow ever reported to use a tool — a behavior previously seen only in chimpanzees and a few other species. The findings were published in Current Biology. Scientists say this challenges our assumptions about cow intelligence. "People are happy to acknowledge that dolphins and so on are extremely clever," said Lindsay Matthews, an animal behavior expert at the University of Auckland. "But I believe most animals are extremely clever if they're given the right task." So why is Veronika special? Researchers think her lifestyle matters. She lives as a pet on a small farm, with plenty of freedom to explore her environment. Most cows on large industrial farms don't get that chance. Still, scientists caution that Veronika is just one cow — her tool use doesn't mean all cows can do it. But it does suggest that we may have underestimated the intelligence of these gentle, hoofed animals. "Somehow Veronika learned to use tools," Osuna-Mascaró said. "And she's doing something that other cows simply can't."
why_it_matters: This discovery challenges how we think about animal intelligence — and reminds us that even animals we overlook, like cows, might be capable of surprising cleverness when given the right environment.

keywords.explanation: The Brown Swiss cow that demonstrated tool use.
An object used to achieve a goal; Veronika uses a brush to scratch.
The long-handled deck brush Veronika manipulates with her tongue.
The country where Veronika lives on a small farm.
The scientific study of animal intelligence and problem-solving.
The scientific journal that published the findings about Veronika.

questions.question: What behavior makes Veronika the first cow to be reported using a tool?
options: She opens gates
She uses a brush to scratch herself
She collects food with a stick
She throws objects at predators
questions.correct_answer: She uses a brush to scratch herself

questions.question: How does Veronika use both ends of the brush?
options: She uses bristles for tough skin and handle for sensitive spots
She eats one end and plays with the other
She uses the handle for scratching and bristles for digging
She switches ends only when the brush breaks
questions.correct_answer: She uses bristles for tough skin and handle for sensitive spots

questions.question: What did Antonio Osuna-Mascaró initially think when Veronika used the handle?
options: That it was a mistake
That she was injured
That she was playing
That it was a learned trick

questions.question: Which species was previously known for similar tool use?
options: Chimpanzees
Dolphins
Elephants
Crows

questions.question: What factor do scientists think helped Veronika learn tool use?
options: Large farm size
Freedom to explore on a small farm
Training from farmers
Hunger
questions.correct_answer: Freedom to explore on a small farm

questions.question: What caution did scientists give about Veronika's ability?
options: It proves all cows can use tools
It does not mean all cows can do it
It only works with blue brushes
It is a sign of illness
questions.correct_answer: It does not mean all cows can do it

background_read: Tool use was once considered a uniquely human trait, but since Jane Goodall's observations in the 1960s, many animals have been seen using tools, including chimpanzees, dolphins, and some birds.
Cows are often underestimated in cognitive research because they are domesticated prey animals, but studies have shown they have good memories and can navigate mazes.
The Messerli Research Institute in Vienna focuses on animal cognition and comparative psychology.
The study of animal tool use helps scientists understand the evolution of intelligence and problem-solving.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Contrast between typical cow behavior and Veronika's unique action
└─ specific: 'When Veronika gets an itch, she doesn't just rub against a fence...'
KEY BEHAVIOR: Veronika uses a brush as a tool, gripping with tongue
├─ DETAIL: She uses both ends (bristles for tough skin, handle for sensitive skin)
└─ ADAPTATION: Sometimes grips middle to switch ends easily
SCIENTIST REACTION: Antonio Osuna-Mascaró initially thought it was a mistake
└─ QUOTE: '...after observing ... so obvious that she was using both tool ends'
IMPLICATION: Challenges assumptions about cow intelligence
└─ QUOTE: Lindsay Matthews – 'most animals are extremely clever if given the right task'
CONTEXT: First cow reported using a tool; published in Current Biology
LIFESTYLE FACTOR: Small farm with freedom to explore
CAVEAT: Veronika is just one cow – not all cows can do this
OPEN QUESTION: What else are cows capable of if given the opportunity?

perspectives.description: This discovery expands our understanding of animal intelligence and suggests that cows, often seen as dull, have cognitive abilities that have been overlooked.
The finding is based on a single animal in a unique environment, so it may not be generalizable. There is a risk of overinterpreting anecdotal observations.

The study adds to the growing evidence that many animals possess sophisticated problem-solving skills when tested in ecologically relevant settings. It highlights the importance of studying animals in enriched environments.
Farmers might rethink housing conditions to allow cows more environmental enrichment, potentially improving welfare and revealing more cognitive abilities. Further systematic studies are needed.
